cited_text: It is probably the defining point of Steve Jobs life because you will see one of the most brilliant entrepreneurs, maybe the greatest entrepreneur to ever live, just make mistake after mistake after mistake.
response_text: The framing that matters: this isn't a story about genius — it's a story about a brilliant person being **wrong**, repeatedly, at scale. That's rarer and more useful to study.

cited_text: Steve wanted grokking from team members, which he describes as full body absorption of the company's DNA.
response_text: He preached deep team loyalty and then turned over **every co-founder**. The gap between the ideal he articulated and the culture he actually created is the whole NeXT story in miniature.

cited_text: Between now and when you have a product, you are the product, my friend. And so you better be nice to people.
response_text: Paul Rand — 30 years older, zero tolerance for nonsense — was the only person who cut through early. **Reputation is the first product.** Jobs ignored this completely.

cited_text: The most important thing you're competing with is not another company, but it is your own ability to execute. He seems to be blissfully unaware that they are unable to execute at the moment.
response_text: Said while the company **couldn't execute at all.** This is the quote that stings most — he could diagnose the disease and still be the cause of it.

cited_text: As time went on, the deep shit list started to look less like an existential priority list and more like Steve's personal wish list. He kept piling on features, adding costs and making last minute demands.
response_text: Scope creep disguised as urgency. He kept **adding features** while the company bled cash and missed every deadline. Classic founder trap: mistaking taste for strategy.

cited_text: Somehow $100,000 had become the company's standard spending unit, as if Steve's logo had set the baseline for what constituted normal expenses.
response_text: This is how **culture actually gets set** — not by what the founder says, but by what the founder spends. The logo fee wasn't just expensive; it was a policy statement.

cited_text: Every operating assumption we had has been proved wrong. The product wasn't ready, the budget has collapsed and the strategy keeps shifting. We are set up for continued failure.
response_text:
Next's internal diagnosis, brutally accurate.
The rare moment someone in the building
said the quiet part loud — and Steve
ignored it anyway.

cited_text:
You know what my mistake was? I gave Steve too much dang money.

When you have too much money, you just don't have that hunger. And you start spending money on floating staircases and $10,000 chairs, which Steve did both of.
response_text:
Ross Perot nails it. Floating staircases
and $10k chairs when you're burning
$39M over budget. Constraints are a
feature, not a bug — Steve needed
scarcity, not a cheque.

cited_text: He was choosing purity over survival.
response_text:
Four words that explain a decade of
bad decisions. The hardware obsession,
the beautiful objects — Steve mistook
aesthetics for strategy. This is the
trap every founder-aesthete risks.

cited_text: Steve did not want to be guided in his thinking. He wanted to know what the facts are on which to base something.
response_text:
Ed Catmull's insight is the practical key
to working with any high-conviction,
fast-talking leader. Don't push — give
them data and wait. Let them arrive.

cited_text: Gil failed to grasp what Steve's 12 years in the wilderness had given him. Steve came away not only with better skills for building technology, but better strategies for getting exactly what he wanted.
response_text:
This is the thesis of the whole episode.
The exile wasn't punishment — it was
graduate school. Gil thought he was
hiring a chastened entrepreneur;
he was handing the keys to a
fully-loaded version.

cited_text: Steve doesn't know how to do anything but lead.
response_text:
Shortest quote, biggest warning.
Everyone around Gil saw it coming.
The only person who didn't was Gil.
